name: Old Rumtek Monastery
adress: Located in Rumtek Village, approximately 2324 km from Gangtok, East Sikkim, India; perched at ~1,500 m altitude
description: The original monastery was founded in the mid-18th century by the 12th Karmapa Lama, Changchub Dorje. It predates the newer Dharma Chakra Centre built in the 1960s. Situated about 2 km from the modern Rumtek Monastery, it features traditional Sikkimese alpine architecturelight blue exteriors, latticed wooden windows, and surrounded by prayer flags
attractions: Main (new) Rumtek Monastery, Karma Shri Nalanda Institute, Golden Stupa,Nehru Botanical Garden:
transport: Reach Gangtok (capital of Sikkim), then take a taxi or shared jeep (~2025 min drive on scenic hilly roads) to Rumtek

name: Rumtek Dharma Chakra Centre
adress: Located about 2324 km from Gangtok, in Rumtek village, East Sikkim (Pin: 737135)
description: The Dharma Chakra Centre (Wheel of Dharma) is the seat-in-exile of the 16th Karmapa, established in 1966 by His Holiness Rangjung Rigpe Dorje with support from the Sikkimese royalty
attractions: kora (circumambulation) along the stupa walkway, Tsomgo Lake, Do Drul Chorten, Hanuman Tok, Banjhakri Falls, and Enchey Monastery
transport: From Gangtok, Rumtek is roughly a 45-minute drive (~2324 km) via scenic, winding roads. Visitors are dropped at the base and must walk uphill (approx. 500 m) to reach the main entrance

name: Enchey Monastery
adress: Perched on a ridge ~3 km northeast of Gangtok city center in East Sikkim, Enchey Monastery overlooks Gangtok with scenic views of the Kanchenjunga range
description: Enchey Monasterymeaning Solitary Monasteryis a sacred 200-year-old Nyingma Buddhist retreat built by Lama Druptob Karpo in a hilltop Chinese-pagoda style. The richly painted prayer hall features carved pillars, murals, and spiritual ambiance. Home to about 90 monks, it houses revered deities and hosts colourful Cham dance festivals. Its legend, spiritual legacy, and sweeping Himalayan views make it a cherished pilgrimage and cultural landmark in Sikkim.
attractions: Located along the road to Nathula, it's close to Gangtoks top sights like Do-Drul Chorten, Hanuman Tok, Rumtek Monastery, and scenic viewpointsperfect for extending your exploration.
transport: Easily accessible from Gangtok via a short taxi ride or a pleasant walk uphill. The monastery is on the GangtokNathu La route. For visitors arriving from afar: Bagdogra Airport (~124 km) or New Jalpaiguri (NJP) railway station (~120 km), followed by a road transfer to Gangtok and then to Enchey.

name: Kathog Dorjeden Monastery (Kartok/Karthok)
adress: Located in Yuksom village, West Sikkim; approx. 32 km from Gangtok near Pakyong foothills. The name "Kartok" stems from Lama Kathok Kuntu Zangpo, instrumental in Sikkims first royal coronation.
description: Kartok Monastery, one of Sikkims oldest Nyingma monasteries, welcomes visitors with its vibrant red, yellow, and gold exterior beside tranquil Kartok Lake. Founded to honor the lama behind Sikkims inaugural coronation, it now offers a calm blend of vibrant art, spiritual rituals, and mountain viewsoffering a deeply peaceful, reflective experience in a rich historical setting.
attractions: Right across lies the sacred Kartok Lake; nearby must-visits include Dubdi Monastery, Norbugang Coronation Throne, and Yuksom village with its town-bazaar vibes and cultural heritage.
transport: Accessible via Yuksom. From New Jalpaiguri (NJP) railhead (~111 km) or Bagdogra Airport (~119 km), take a taxi/shared jeep to Yuksom, then a short walk to the monastery. Best months to visit: MarchJune.

name: Lingdum (Zurmang) Monastery
adress: Lingdum Monastery is situated at Lingdum (Ranka), Sikkim  roughly 1620 km east of Gangtok (about an hours drive), on forested alpine terrain.
description: Lingdum Monastery, completed in 1999 under the guidance of Zurmang Gharwang Rinpoche, belongs to the Zurmang Kagyu sect of Tibetan Buddhism. Set across 21 acres of alpine forest, it showcases a five-storey prayer hall housing a giant 5 m gilded Buddha statue alongside Guru Rinpoche. Walls adorned with intricate thangkas and frescoes surround an expansive courtyard. It doubles as a training center for novice monks and hosts vivid mask dances during festivals like Gutor
attractions: Banjhakri Falls, Nirvana Café
transport: Easily reached by taxi or shared jeep from Gangtok (~16 km; ~45 min). The route continues past Banjhakri Fallsmany visitors combine both in one trip

name: Lingdok Tsangkhar Monastery
adress: Lingdok Tsangkhar Monastery, an all-women monastic center, is located in Lingdok village, East Sikkimperched amid terraced fields overlooking serene Himalayan landscapes near Gangtok. Its a tranquil spiritual retreat distinguished by its female stewardship and peaceful setting.
description: Lingdok Tsangkhar Monastery is a unique spiritual enclave managed entirely by women monks, offering visitors serene meditation, chanting, and immersive prayer experiences. The monasterys position amid Lingdoks rolling rice terraces provides panoramic views and natural calm. Though relatively small, it holds rich cultural and religious significance, fostering awareness and empowerment through its women-led spiritual community.
attractions: Fambong Lho Wildlife Sanctuary
transport: Reachable via a scenic walk or short drive through forests and rice terraces from nearby Pangthang or Gangtok outskirts. Many visitors start from Taj Guras Kutir Resort, walking through cedar and pine woods to the monastery. Available transport includes local jeeps or taxis to Pangthang, followed by a guided walk

name: Simig Monastery
adress: Simig Monastery  also spelled Simik Monastery  is situated near Singtam town in East Sikkim, nestled amidst lush green hillsides not far from Gangtok. This tranquil hilltop location offers a quiet retreat into Buddhist reflection.
description: Simig Monastery, set against the serene backdrop of East Sikkims rolling terrain, radiates peaceful simplicity. Constructed in 1843, the Nyingma sect temple offers quiet meditation, spiritual ambience, and panoramic views that bring calm to visitors and pilgrims alike.
attractions: Singtam town, Rinak Monastery, Pabyuk Monastery, Lingdok Tsangkhar Monastery
transport: Accessible via road from Singtam. Travelers from Gangtok or New Jalpaiguri (NJP)/Bagdogra can take a taxi or shared jeep to Singtam, followed by a short walk or local ride to Simig Monastery nestled on nearby hills.

name: Singtam Monastery
adress: Singtam Gumpa is perched on a hillside just a short walk uphill from Singtam town in East Sikkim, overlooking the confluence of the Teesta and Rangpo rivers, all framed by forested ridges.
description: Singtam Gumpa is perched on a hillside just a short walk uphill from Singtam town in East Sikkim, overlooking the confluence of the Teesta and Rangpo rivers, all framed by forested ridges.
attractions: Tendong Hill viewpoint, Shiva/Kali temple in Sumin Reserve Forest, Singtam Bazaar & local market, Riverside walks along Teesta/Rangpo, Dzongu Valley
transport: From Gangtok (~30 km), hire a taxi or shared jeep to Singtam (~1 hour). Upon arrival in Singtam, a short uphill walk from the town center takes you to the monastery.

name: Tsangek Monastery
adress: Tsangek Monasteryestablished in 1888 AD by Lama Tsewang Galpo of the Drukpa Kagyu traditionis located a few kilometers from Pakyong town in East Sikkims Pakyong district
description: A serene pilgrimage site, Tsangek Monastery delivers spiritual calm and rustic charm typical of eastern Himalayan monastic architecture. Founded in the late 19th century, it reflects Drukpa Kagyu heritage and is valued less for grandeur and more for peaceful ambience set against the backdrop of Pakyongs lush hills.
opening: from dawn to dusk with no entry fee
attractions: Pabyuk Monastery, Lingdok Tsangkhar Monastery, Rinak Monastery
transport: short drive from Pakyong townthe route offers scenic views through East Sikkim hills. Most travelers will approach from Gangtok via Pakyong Airport (road travel), or via New Jalpaiguri (NJP) railway, then onward to Pakyong and the monastery.

name: Tsulakhang (Royal Chapel) Monastery
adress: Located within the Royal Palace complex on Ridge Road, Gangtok, Sikkim, Indiajust about 3 km from Gangtok SNT Bus Station
description: The Royal Chapel of the Chogyals, built in 1898 AD, is a Buddhist palatial monastery that served as the venue for royal coronations, marriages, and court ceremonies. Its two-story assembly hall houses a vast collection of Buddhist scriptures, richly painted murals, intricate altars, Snow Lion sculptures, and tantric deities. It remains a central place of worship and cultural heritage in Gangtok.
attractions: Namgyal Institute of Tibetology, Do-Drul Chorten, Ganesh Tok, Tsogmo (Tsomgo) Lake, Cable Car ride, Himalayan Zoological Park
transport: Walkable or a short taxi ride (~10 min) from Gangtok town center. Accessible via local buses or jeeps. Best route: from Bagdogra Airport (~125 km) or New Jalpaiguri railway station, proceed via Gangtok to the palace area.

name: Amba Mamring Monastery
adress: near, Legship-Rabongla Rd, Sikkim 737139
description: Amba Mamring Monastery is located in East Sikkim, established in 1929. It is listed among the states monasteries in official records.
attractions: Reshi Hot Springs,Lho Kando Sang Phug (Cave of the Occult Fairies),Buddha Park (Tathagata Tsal)
transport: first reach Siliguri or New Jalpaiguri (NJP) in West Bengal by flight, train, or bus, and then take a shared jeep or private taxi from there into Sikkim to Rabongla or the surrounding Legship area

name: Bakcham Monastery
adress: East Sikkim (exact location not publicly available)
description: Namgyal Monastery, also known as Pal Ewam Namgyal Choedhe Thupten Dhargyeling Monastery, has stood for nearly a thousand years as a spiritual and cultural pillar of Mustang. The monastery took its present The monastery has faced decline but has been revitalized through the efforts of various lamas, particularly H.E. Chogye Trichen Rinpoche and Khenpo Tsewang Rigzinform when Ngorchen Kunga Zangpo, a great master of the Sakya tradition, established it as a monastic school. Over the centuries, it has been shaped and preserved by some of the greatest Buddhist scholars and practitioners, including Jamyang Sherab Gyatso, Panchen Shakya Chokden, and Lowo Khenchen, the founder of Mustangs Buddhist tradition.
attractions: (not sure)

name: Bongyong Ani Gonpa
adress: this is an Ani (Nun) Monastery within the range of Gangtok, East Sikkim.
description: Ms. Kesang Chungyalpa with her noble intention to foster the Buddha Dharma founded it in 2005.It was given a religious name as ?Khachoed Pemayulling? It follows Nyingmapa Sect of Tibetan Buddhism and is being functioned under the spiritual guidance of Ven. Khamtrul Rinpoche.As one of the newer nunneries in Sikkims rich Buddhist landscape, Bongyong Ani Gonpa provides a female-centric space for spiritual practice and monastic life within the Nyingma tradition. Though details are limited, its foundation highlights ongoing growth in female-led Buddhist communities. Founded in 2005, it reflects modern continuation of an age-old tradition
attractions: not sure
transport: not sure

name: Burtuk Ugen Pemacholing Monastery
adress: Burtuk, Gangtok, East Sikkim, Sikkim, India
description: The monastery serves as a spiritual center for the Gurung and Tamu communities, offering a place for meditation, prayer, and cultural activities. It is also known for its monastic school, which provides religious education to young monks and nuns. The monastery is situated in a serene location, offering panoramic views of the surrounding mountains and valleys.
attractions: Tsomgo Lake, Baba Mandir
transport: From Gangtok, Burtuk is approximately 7 km away and can be reached by taxi or local buses. The nearest airport is Pakyong Airport (PYG), approximately 35 km from Gangtok.

name: Choten Monastery
adress: 8J73+4WP, M.P.Golai, Tadong, Gangtok, Sikkim 737102
description: Choten (Chhoedten) Monastery was started with a small residential Lhakhang built by H.E. Trulshig Rinpoche in 1946, which is situated in the premises of Deorali Dudul Chhoedten. Later Another Venerable. High Nyingmapa Lama called Dodrubchen Rinpoche came to this place in 1959 from Tibet. He gradually developed this small Lhakhang into a well-established Monastery with enrolment of 400 to 500 Monks. Built in 194546 by Trulshik Rinpoche of the Nyingma school, Do-Drul Chorten is one of Gangtoks most significant religious sites. It houses sacred relics including the Dorjee Phurba mandala, Kangyur scriptures, and mantras. Surrounded by 108 prayer wheels, visitors can join local devotees in circumnavigationa serene and spiritually uplifting experience amid gentle chanting and colorful prayer flags
attractions: Namgyal Institute of Tibetology, Enchey Monastery, Do-Drul Chorten itself is centralclosely surrounded by Gangtoks spiritual landmarks.
transport: Easily accessible by taxi or shared jeep from Gangtok town. Expect a short uphill walk (~500 m) from the base to reach the chorten

name: Dichen Choling Monastery
adress: Dechen Choling Monastery, Sichey, Gangtok, East Sikkim, Sikkim, India
description: The monastery serves as a spiritual center for the Tamang community in Gangtok, offering a place for meditation, prayer, and cultural activities. It is situated in a serene location, providing panoramic views of the surrounding mountains and valleys. The monastery's architecture reflects traditional Tibetan style, and it houses sacred texts and religious artifacts.
attractions: Tsomgo Lake
transport: From Gangtok, Sichey is approximately 7 km away and can be reached by taxi or local buses.

name: Dolepchen Boudha Sanskrit Monastery
adress: Dolepchen, East Sikkim, India
description: Dolepchen Boudha Sanskrit Monastery is a Buddhist monastery located in Dolepchen, East Sikkim, India. Founded in 1991 through the collective efforts of the local community and with support from the state government, the monastery serves as a center for spiritual practice and cultural preservation. It is affiliated with the Nyingma tradition of Tibetan Buddhism. The monastery is known for its serene environment, traditional architecture, and the practice of various Buddhist rituals and ceremonies. It plays a significant role in the religious and cultural life of the local community, offering a space for meditation, prayer, and religious education.
attractions: beautiful sight seeing, peaceful enviornment
transport: From Gangtok, Dolepchen is approximately 34 km away and can be reached by taxi or local buses.

name: Duchi Gyalton Monastery
adress: Lower Sichey, Gangtok, East Sikkim, Sikkim, India
description: This monastery is situated at lower Sichey, Gangtok, East Sikkim. It was built in 2003 by Ven. Tulku Thinlay Dawa Topden on the land donated bt Prince Wangchuk Namgyal. The main Altar contains statues of Late Gyalton Rinpoche alongwith a Chhoedten containing his relics. The staues and the chhoedten were previously housed in Tsuklakhang  monastery, Gangtok from where it was shifted to this monastery. The Monastery presently has two senior monks and ten novice monks and belongs to the Karma Kagyupa School.
attractions: Kabi Lungchok, Ranka Monastery
transport: From Gangtok, Lower Sichey is approximately 3 km away and can be reached by taxi or local buses.

name: Kagon Tshechhogling Monastery
adress: Located in Sikkim, India, established in 1977 AD under the Kagyu lineage. Exact village or district not publicly documented.
description: The Chinese occupation of Tibet in 1959 forced the monks of this monastery to leave Tibet.All the spiritual properties brought from Yatung were housed in the present Gonpa at Chandmari, East Sikkim. At the initial stage, this Toedruk Kagyu Centre was temporarily established in 1977 in the private house of Thongyal Lama and  was being run with the help of the devotees.Later in 1987, the Committee purchased a building and converted it into a Kagyu Gonpa.Since then religious ceremonies are regularly held in the monastery on all the auspicious days. Every year, Gutor ritual like other monasteries is held with mask dances. The monasteries of Renock, Tsangyek and Pabyuk of Sikkim and the monasteries of Lingsay, Tangtha and Poedong monasteries in West Bengal have an affiliation to the Kagon Tshechhogling Monastery of Chandmari. The above monasteries depute their monks annually to the Kagon Tshechhogling to participate in the annual Gutor ceremony in the 8th lunar month and Tangyur reading in the first lunar month as a tribute to this monastery. Lt. Kagyu Tulku was their spiritual Head and 7th in the lineage. They follow the Kagyupa Sect of Toedruk Lineage of Tibetan Buddhism.
attractions: not sure
transport: not sure

name: Khatek Pema Choling Monastery
adress: East Sikkim, listed as Khatek Pema Choling Monasteryestablished in 1985, Kagyu tradition
description: This is a small Karma Kagyupa Monastery located in Asam Lingzey village, East Sikkim. It was built in the year 1985 with Government aid and is supported by public contribution. The place was simply a hermitage site earlier where Monks go for retreat courses.Later, people of the area, faced difficulty fetching Lamas from far off places, for their religious services. Thus they unanimously decided to build a Monastery at this holy site. After completion of the monastery the management Committee engaged one Master Lama, who has been deputed from Rumtek Monastery, for recruiting the young monks to study & practice Buddhist rituals. At present, the monastery has more than 30 monks and functions like other monasteries.
attractions: not sure
transport: not sure

name: Martam Namdzong Monastery
adress: Martam Namdzong Monastery  located in Martam (Rakdong-Tintek area), East Sikkim
description: Martam Namzong Monastery is situated at few kilometers from Rumtek Gonpa and follows Karma Kagyupa sect. It was initially a Manilhakhang built during the then Chhogyal Sir Thutob Namgyal (1874-1914). Later it was developed into a small Gonpa in 1917 A.D.H.H. 16th Gyalwa Karmapa also paid a visit to this monastery and blessed with new name as Karma Tashi Lhundub Chokhorling . The old monastery was small and was in a dilapidated condition due to exposure to natural elements and lack of maintenance. Therefore, the Lamas with an intention to extend the size, rebuilt the monastery in the year 1994 with Government aid and is yet to be completed.
attractions: Surrounding Martam is rich in spiritual and natural sites, including Rumtek Monastery and Sang Monastery, about 6 km away; Nehru Botanical Park; Phambongla Wildlife Sanctuary (treks, birding, nature walks)
transport: Martam is approximately 34 km from Gangtok, reachable by road via Rangpo ? Ranipul ? Rumtek

name: Pathing Matsang Monastery
adress: Matsang village, Pathing area, East Sikkim.
description: Originally established in 1860 by Lama Lharib Dawa and reconstructed in 2005 after earthquake damage, this Nyingma sect monastery blends tradition and resilience. It continues to serve as a cultural and spiritual center with historic roots and recent restoration This monastery was said to have been shifted from Lingkoed Village to its present site i.e. Matsang and re-established it in 1860 A.D by a Lama called Lharib Dawa who named it Linkoed Matsang Rinchen Tse Gonpa". The Lamas rebuilt the present monastery in 1982 due to structural damages caused by an earthquake. However, it was remodeled in 2005 with government aid and renamed as Pathing Matsang Rinchhen Tse Gonpa since the monastery is properly located on a small hillock of Matsang village within Pathing area. The original monastery at Lingkoed was founded by a Lama called Drubwang PemaTsondu. It belongs to Nyingmapa Sect of Buddhism. Today it has become one of the major monasteries in East Sikkim and functions all the ceremonies with annual mask dance in winter.
attractions: Thambi Viewpoint,Elephant Lake (Bitan Cho)
transport: Drive to Gangtok, the capital, via National Highway 31A, which is a scenic route that follows the Teesta River. Once in Gangtok, you can hire a taxi to take you to Matsang village, which is located in the Pathing area within East Sikkim.

name: Radong Tensung Monastery
adress: Radong, East Sikkim, India
description: Radong monastery is a Lepcha Buddhist monastery founded by Lama Rabden Nigit in 1959. Earlier it was a branch monastery of Phensang Monastery, North.The name of the monastery is derived from the name of the hill, which resembles the head of a goat, on which this Monastery is located. Presently the monastery has 60 lamas and is capable of functioning the all monastic activities. The monastery belongs to Nyingmapa Sect and functions all the major ceremonies with annual mask dance.
attractions: Tashi view, Ganesh Tok
transport: From Gangtok, Radong is approximately 30 km away and can be reached by taxi or local buses.

name: Raloong Monastery (East)
adress: 88HP+93X, Sikkim 737139
description: This monastery is located at Raloong village in the block of West Pandam, East Sikkim. Earlier, the people of this area were the devotees of Simig monastery East Sikkim which is at a very long walking distance from Raloong.It was very troublesome and inconvenient for the devotees to walk such distances to get in touch with  the lamas at Simig Monastery whenever there was a death in their area. Seeing the difficulties faced by the devotees, a Lama called Ugen Chhodak,  decided to construct a branch monastery of his parent Gonpa  to cater the religious needs of the people of Raloong. Therefore, he built a small Gonpa in 1956 A.D. with an enrolment of 8 monks. It follows the Nyingmapa Sect. Presently a new the monastery is being rebuilt just behind the old one.
attractions: Buddha Park (Tathagata Tsal), Ralong Hot Springs, Ravangla viewpoint, and lush forest trails.
transport: Accessible by taxi or shared jeep from Ravangla town (~6 km). Visitors from Gangtok or NJP/Bagdogra travel via road to Ravangla and proceed to the monastery. A scenic uphill route leads to the entrance.

name: Ray Mindu Katenling Monastery
adress: Ray Mindu Katenling Monasteryalso called Rey Monasteryis perched atop a hill between Rumtek and Ranka in East Sikkim, with approximate coordinates 27°19?12? N, 88°33?37? E.
description: The monastery was originally established in 1873 by the Kagyupa followers under the name "Ray Mindu Katenling Monastery." and is locally also known as Rey Monastery. It is located atop a hill between Rumtek and Ranka Monastery. Recently road has been built upto the monastery. In recent times during its process of rebuilding it was renamed as Ray Mindu Katen Namgyaling Gonpa. The followers are primarily from Lepcha community who perform all major Buddhist ceremonies throughout the year.
attractions: Rumtek Dharma Chakra Centre, Lingdum (Ranka) Monastery, Ranka landscapes
transport: Accessible via a newly built road through the Rey village hills. Best approached from Gangtok via Rumtek, with local taxis or shared jeeps available; some walking may be required from the road terminus.

name: Rinak Monastery
adress: Rinak Monastery, a Barawa Kagyu lineage temple, is located in East Sikkim, approximately at coordinates 27.1860° N, 88.6776° E
description: Established in the 17th century by Konchok Gyeltsen, a disciple of the Third Barawa Tulku, Rinak Monastery is a historic Barawa Kagyu institution. It played a key role in introducing the Barawa tradition to Sikkim. The monastery is also noted as the site where the Seventh Kagyu Tulku passed away in 2007
attractions: Do-Drul Chorten, and Ganesh Tok and Hanuman Tok
transport: For visiting or transport details, contacting the Sikkim Ecclesiastical Department or local guides in East Sikkim is recommended

name: Samdong Mintokgang Monastery
adress: Samdong Mintokgang Monastery is located in Samdong (Rakdong Tintek block), East Sikkim, India
description: Samdong Mintokgang Monastery was built in 1913 A.D. and it was a branch monastery of Labrang Gonpa in North Sikkim. A Buddha Statue was gifted to this monastery by Chogyal Palden Thondup Namgyal.It is said that when the statue was installed here all the illness that was prevailing in the area were cured. It was repaired in 1977 but later dismantled  and rebuilt with government aid in 1989-90.H.ETingkyed Gonjang Rinpoche blessed it with Rabney ceremony in 1992. Earlier, Labrang Monastery used to depute Ponlob (Tutor) Lama to supervise the religious ceremonies of this Gonpa. Now, its own lamas are capable of running the monastery and perform all the ceremonies throughout the year.
attractions: Ban Jhakri Falls Park, Gonjang Monastery, Lingdum (Ranka) Monastery
transport: Accessible via Samdong village, reached by road from Gangtok or Singtam. Visitors typically travel by taxi or shared jeep to Samdong, then a short walk leads to the monastery.

name: Sang Monastery
adress: Located in Sanga Village, East Sikkimnear Rumtek and approximately 23 km from Gangtok.
description: Established in 1912 AD by Sang Athing, on land he donated for spiritual community purposes.
attractions: Rumtek Monastery, Sanga Village scenic trails
transport: Accessible by road via Rumtekapproximately 23 km from Gangtok

name: Sang-Ngor Monastery
adress: Located in the Upper Tathangchen area of Gangtok, Sikkim, approximately 5 km from the town center. Founded in 1961 by Luding Khen Rinpoche
description: The only Sakya-order monastery in Sikkim, Sang-Ngor is a vibrant spiritual retreat featuring colorful murals, thangkas, a prominent Shakyamuni Buddha statue, and prayer flags adorning a stupa. Founded with blessings from His Holiness the Dalai Lama and HH Sakya Trizin, it serves as a center for Tibetan refugee monastics and rituals like summer retreats (Yarney).
attractions: Tashi Viewpoint, Gonjang Monastery, Banjhakri Falls, Gangtok city
transport: Easily accessible via taxi or shared jeep from Gangtok, about a 2030 minute drive uphill to the hilltop location

name: Sumon Thubten Gatsalling Monastery
adress: Sumon is a Lepcha word and is a name of a Village just situated opposite hill of the Sang hill range, sikkim India.
description: History:This was monastery originally started with a Manilhakhang belonging to the Karma Kagyupa Sect and was established in wood-mouse year of 15th Rabjung cycle corresponding to 1924. It was founded by Lama Ngodub as a branch monastery of Phodang Gonpa, North Sikkim. It was later converted into a Gonpa in 1966. The main altar has a statue of the 16th Gyalwa Karmapa donated by Ruktek Gonpa and a statue of Buddha presented by Kharwang Tulku. It functions all the ceremonies throughout.

name: Taktse Ani Gonpa
adress: Buddhist nunnery in Penlong (Gangtok block), East Sikkim.
description:
Taktse Ugen Chokhorling Ani Gonpa is situated approximately five kilometers from Gangtok, East Sikkim. The monastery follows Nyingmapa Sect. In early 20th century, a Lama called Amje Lama came from the land of Ladak and built a small hermitage at present Taktse where he spent the rest of his life in retreat courses. He ,being an Amje, used to treat the patients with Tibetan medicine and thereby many householders of the surrounding village such as Luying, bojogari and Pellong became the devotees of Taktse Tshamkhang. After the demise of the said Lama, his daughter Ani Damchho Doma (Nun) carried further her fathers heritage. Later, Ani Damchho Doma founded a small Gonpa just below the old Hermitage in 1980 with the help of contributions from the devotees and the financial grant from the Government. She established this Ani Gonpa giving the name as Ugen Chokhorling Gonpa with the enrolment of 12 nuns and carried out all the periodical pujas like the other monasteries.
Unfortunately, Ani Damchho passed away in an early age and her sister Ani Ganga Lhamo took over the charge of this Gonpa who in recent years, constructed a small Dubkhang or meditation Centre for  the nuns to go for Losum-Dasum, which is a three years and three months retreat course
attractions: not sure
transport: not sure

name: Tingkye Gonjang Monastery
adress: Situated at Fatak Bojhogari near Tashi Viewpoint, approximately 68 km from Gangtok town center in East Sikkim. Located at an elevation of about 6,066 ft (?1,850 m)
description: Founded in 1981 by H.E. Tingkye Gonjang Rinpoche, recognized as an incarnation of the 15th-century Nyingma tertön, Yolmo Ngakchang. Gonjang Monastery follows the Jangter tradition of the Nyingma school and offers a nine-year curriculum in monastic, Tibetan, and English studies. Its richly adorned interiorsincluding statues of the three founding fathers (Khen-lop Cho-Sum) and the 25 disciples of Guru Padmasambhavatogether with tranquil ambiance and thoughtful architecture, make it both a nurturing spiritual learning center and a serene escape near Gangtok
attractions: Tashi Viewpoint, Rumtek Monastery, Banjhakri Falls
transport: Accessible by taxi or shared jeep from Gangtok via Sichey and Lower Burtuk Road, then NH10just 600 m beyond Tashi Viewpoint. The drive takes roughly 2030 minutes from central Gangtok

name: Karthok Monastery (alt. Kartok)
adress: Kartok Monastery is situated in Yuksom village, West Sikkimperched opposite the sacred Kartok Lake. Founded in the 17th century in memory of Lama Kathok Kuntu Zangpo by Chogyal Thutob Namgyal, it is considered the sixth oldest monastery in Sikkim and belongs to the Nyingma tradition.
description: Kartok Monastery is a vibrant red-and-gold Nyingma shrine with finely crafted Tibetan-style architecture, set against lush forested hills. Linked to the coronation site of Sikkim's first king, it exudes peaceful spiritual energy. The adjacent Kartok Lake reflects both the monastery and mountain vistasmaking it a meditative and photogenic retreat steeped in history.
attractions: Dubdi Monastery, Ngadak Chörling Monastery, Kartok Lake, Norbugang Coronation Throne
transport: Reachable via Yuksom village, which is accessible by road from Gangtok, Pelling, or Geyzing. The nearest airport is Bagdogra (~119 km), and railway access is through New Jalpaiguri (NJP) (~111 km). From Yuksom, the monastery is a short walk from the center, nestled by Kartok Lake.

name: Rhenock Monastery
adress: Rhenock (Rhenock Gumpa) is located near Rhenock town in Pakyong district, East Sikkim.
description: A picturesque Buddhist monastery nestled on a hillside along the historical Silk Route, Rhenock Monastery features ornate architecture, peaceful surroundings, and vibrant prayer flagsmaking it a favorite for spiritual retreats and photographers
attractions: Aritar Lake, Ram Gauri Sangralaya Museum, Siddhi Vinayak (Ganesh) Temple, Rhenock Bazaar
transport: Accessible via road from Gangtok (~63 km) or Pakyong Airport (~31 km). The nearest railhead is New Jalpaiguri (NJP), about ~104 km away. Regular taxis or SNT buses service Rhenock

name: Simik Monastery
adress: Simik Monastery is located near Singtam town in East Sikkim, nestled amid serene hillside terrain overlooking valley landscapes.
description: A tranquil and lesser-known Buddhist monastery, Simik Monastery offers meditative calm away from tourist crowds. Surrounded by verdant hills, it is ideal for quiet reflection and spiritual immersionwith unobstructed panoramic views and a peaceful rural ambiance.
attractions: Singtam town, local prayer flaglined walks, Sitings within East Sikkim spiritual loop
transport: Accessible via road from Singtam. Travelers from Gangtok or New Jalpaiguri (NJP)/Bagdogra can take a taxi or shared jeep to Singtam, followed by a short walk or local transport to the monastery

name: Phensang Monastery
adress: CJC6+73J, Sikkim 737116 (Phensang, North District, Sikkim, India)
description:
Phensang Monastery stands as a serene testament to the spiritual heritage of this beautiful state. Established in 1721 during the reign of Jigme Pawo, it is a place that exudes peace and tranquility, inviting travelers to step away from the beaten path and immerse themselves in a world of meditative calm.

As you approach Phensang Monastery, the first thing that strikes you is its majestic setting. Perched on a gentle slope with a panoramic view of the surrounding mountains, the monastery seems to be in harmony with nature. The architecture is a splendid example of the traditional Buddhist style, with vibrant colors and intricate designs that capture the eye and the imagination.
attractions: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via SingtamChungthang Road (~29 km, 1 hour). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (56 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (120 km).

name: Phodong Monastery
adress: Phodong, North Sikkim, Sikkim, India
description: Built in the early 18th century the Phodong Monastery is one of the most significant monasteries in Sikkim. Its stunning architecture adorned with vibrant murals and intricate wooden carvings speaks volumes about the rich cultural and spiritual heritage of the region. As you walk through its halls the faint sound of monks chanting adds to the mystical aura of the place. The annual Cham dance festival is a must see if youre lucky to visit during that time. The vibrant rituals colorful masks and traditional music bring the monastery to life offering a unique glimpse into Sikkims traditions. When you book your journey with your tours and travel you can plan your trip to coincide with this incredible celebration. The best way to experience the Phodong Monastery is with a guided trip organized by your tours and travel ensuring you dont miss nearby attractions like Labrang Monastery and Tumlong Palace. These sites complement your visit enriching your understanding of the areas cultural significance.
attractions: Phensang Monastery, Kabi Longstok, Yumthang Valley, Lachung, Seven Sisters Waterfall, Tashi View Point, Lachen Village
transport: Located just 38 kilometers from Gangtok the journey takes you through scenic roads that wind through lush hills and picturesque landscapes.

name: Tholung Monastery
adress: Dzongu, North Sikkim, Sikkim, India
description: Tholung Monastery, established in 1716 by Chogyal Phuntsog Namgyal, is one of the most sacred monasteries in Sikkim. It is a repository of rare manuscripts, ancient relics, and Buddhist artifacts, many of which are preserved in a controlled environment. The monastery is maintained by the state government and is open to visitors only during certain festival periods to protect its heritage
opening: Open mainly during MarchApril and SeptemberOctober for specific festivals; otherwise access is restricted.
attractions: Dzongu Valley, Kabi Longstok, Phodong Monastery, Yumthang Valley, Seven Sisters Waterfall
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via ManganDzongu Road (~65 km, 34 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~80 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~150 km). Trekking is required for final access to the monastery.

name: Tsungthang (Chungthang) Monastery
adress: Chungthang, North Sikkim, Sikkim, India
description: Tsungthang Monastery, also known as Chungthang Monastery, is a historic Buddhist monastery situated at the confluence of the Lachen and Lachung rivers. It follows the Nyingmapa tradition and has been an important center for meditation and spiritual learning in North Sikkim. The monastery features traditional Tibetan architecture, prayer halls, and houses a community of resident monks.
attractions: Lachung Village, Lachen Village, Yumthang Valley, Seven Sisters Waterfall, Phodong Monastery, Gurudongmar Lake
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via North Sikkim Highway (~118 km, 56 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~120 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~150 km).

name: Labrang Monastery (North)
adress: Near Kyongsa Village, North Sikkim, Sikkim, India
description: Labrang Monastery is a small but important Nyingmapa Buddhist monastery in North Sikkim. It serves as a meditation and study center for monks and preserves the local Tibetan Buddhist traditions. The monastery is known for its serene surroundings, traditional architecture, and a tranquil environment ideal for spiritual practice.
attractions: Kyongsa Village, Lachung Village, Yumthang Valley, Seven Sisters Waterfall, Gurudongmar Lake, Chungthang Monastery
transport: From Gangtok, take a taxi or jeep via North Sikkim Highway to Lachung, then proceed to Kyongsa Village (~120 km, 56 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~130 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~150 km).

name: Ringyim Monastery
adress: Above Mangan Bazaar, North Sikkim, India
description: Ringyim Monastery, also known as Rigdzin Tharling Gonpa, was established in 1852. It is a significant Lepcha Buddhist monastery following the Nyingmapa tradition. The monastery is located in a serene setting and serves as a spiritual center for the local community.
attractions: Dzongu Valley, Tholung Monastery, Lingthem Monastery, Kabi Longstok, Yumthang Valley
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via Mangan Road (~65 km, 34 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~70 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~120 km).

name: Lachung Monastery
adress: Lachung, North Sikkim, India
description: Lachung Monastery, also known as Samten Cheoling Monastery, was established in 1880 and follows the Nyingmapa sect of Tibetan Buddhism. Situated on the banks of the Lachung River, it is one of the oldest monasteries in North Sikkim. The monastery houses statues of Guru Padmasambhava, Chenrezig, and other deities, along with sacred texts like the Kangyur and Tengyur.
attractions: Yumthang Valley, Zero Point, Rhododendron Valley, Lachen Monastery, Gurudongmar Lake
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via North Sikkim Highway (~118 km, 56 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~120 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~150 km).

name: Lingthem Gonpa
adress: Lingthem, Dzongu Valley, North Sikkim, India
description: Lingthem Monastery, also known as Chophel Dargyeling, was established in 1857 as a branch of Pemayangtse Monastery in West Sikkim. It serves as a spiritual center for the local Lepcha community and follows the Nyingmapa tradition of Tibetan Buddhism. The monastery is situated in a serene location, offering panoramic views of the surrounding mountains and valleys.
attractions: Tholung Monastery, Dzongu Valley, Kabi Longstok, Yumthang Valley, Seven Sisters Waterfall
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via ManganDzongu Road (~65 km, 34 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~70 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~120 km).

name: Lachen Monastery
adress: Lachen, North Sikkim, India
description: Lachen Monastery, also known as Ngodub Choling Monastery, was established in 1858 by Lama Karchen Dorje Drak, a disciple of Jamyang Choki Rinzing. Originally a small hut with eight monks, it has evolved into a significant spiritual center. The monastery is renowned for its 1000-handed Chenrezig statue and traditional Tibetan architecture. It was fully reconstructed in 1977 with financial assistance from the state government. Lachen Ngodub Choling Monastery is located about 28 kms from Tsunthang Sub Division Office. It was built in 1858 A.D. with 8 monks. This Gonpa was first a small hut by built by Lama Karchen Dorje Drak, who was a student of Jamyang Choki Rinzing who in turn was a disciple of Terdag Lingpa Of Tibet.The lama stayed in this Gonpa for three years and commissioned a clay statue of the 1000 handed Chenrezig for the Gonpa before returning to Tibet. Later, Gelong Gonpu from Solo Khombu, Nepal came here. He was a student of Lama Karchen Dorje Drak. During his stay the old hut was dismantled and replaced with a new mud structure in the Earth dog year. He also stayed for three years and returned to Nepal. During this period the Gonpa had 10 monks.
attractions: Yumthang Valley, Zero Point, Rhododendron Valley, Gurudongmar Lake, Lachung Monastery
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via North Sikkim Highway (~107 km, 45 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~120 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~150 km).

name: Tingbung Monastery
adress: Tingbung Village, Upper Dzongu, North Sikkim, India
description: Tingbung Monastery, also known as Rigdzin Tharling Gonpa, was established in 1843 by Lama Tambi. It follows the Nyingmapa tradition of Tibetan Buddhism and serves as a spiritual center for the Lepcha community in Dzongu. The monastery is situated in a serene location, offering panoramic views of the surrounding mountains and valleys.
attractions: Dzongu Valley, Tholung Monastery, Lingzya Waterfall, Keushong Lake
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via ManganDzongu Road (~65 km, 34 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~70 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~120 km).

name: Ship Kunzang Choling Monastery
adress: Shipgyer Village, North Sikkim, India
description: Ship Kunzang Choling Monastery, established in 1900, is situated in one of the most remote and challenging-to-access areas of North Sikkim. The monastery was originally built in 1900, and the present building was constructed in 1985. It was completed in 7 years and was named Ship Kunzang Choeling Monastery. The monastery is located in a difficult-to-access area of North Sikkim and came into existence in 1900.
attractions: Dzongu Valley, Tholung Monastery, Lingzya Waterfall, Keushong Lake
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via ManganDzongu Road (~65 km, 34 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~70 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~120 km). Access to Shipgyer Village requires a 4WD vehicle due to challenging terrain.

name: Hee Gyathang Monastery
adress: Hee Gyathang, Upper Dzongu, North Sikkim, India
description: Hee Gyathang Monastery, established in 1914 by hermit Abi Putso Rangdrol, is situated in a secluded hilltop location at an altitude of 1,669 meters. The monastery is accessible via a one-hour trek through dense forests from Hee Gyathang village. It follows the Nyingmapa tradition of Tibetan Buddhism and serves as a spiritual center for the local Lepcha community.
attractions: Dzongu Valley, Tholung Monastery, Lingzya Waterfall, Keushong Lake.
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via ManganDzongu Road (~65 km, 34 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~70 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~120 km). Access to Hee Gyathang village requires a 4WD vehicle due to challenging terrain.

name: Tareng Gonpa
adress: Upper Dzongu, North Sikkim, Sikkim, India
description: Tareng Dargye Choling monastery was earlier called as 'Gor Tareng Dargye Choling Gonpa' since the devotees from both Gor and Tareng village attend to the same Gonpa for performing periodical Pujas. It was founded in1929 A.D. led by a Lama called Choying Lepcha and built it with the help of the devotees of both the villages.The monastery is properly located at Tareng. Gor village is bid far away from Tareng. However, in the year 2002 A.D, The devotee of Gor village, in view of the long distance between their villages and Gonpa, decided to establish a Gonpa of their own. Thereafter, it came to be known as Tareng Dargye Choling Gonpa
attractions: The area contains villages like Lingdem, which has hot water springs and waterfalls
transport: As a restricted area, a valid permit is mandatory to enter and explore Dzongu.  It is approximately 70 km from Gangtok and takes about 3.5 hours to reach by road.

name: Shagyong Monastery
adress: Shagyong, North Sikkim, India
description: Shagyong Gonpa in North Sikkim was earlier known as Sangtok-Shagyong Gonpa since devotees from Sangtok Village also attend to this monastery. At present, Sangtok has small Manilhakhang of their own.It was initially founded by a Lama called Dechen Lhundub in 1940 A.D. However, the lamas, with a view to extend its size, dismantled the old structure and started rebuilding in the year 1982 with government aid. But, as a sorry state of affairs, the committee could not carry further the work beyond its foundation level and left it as it was till now, due lack of funds.
attractions: Sangtok Manilakhang, Dzongu Valley, Tholung Monastery, Lingzya Waterfall, Keushong Lake.
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via ManganDzongu Road (~65 km, 34 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~70 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~120 km). Access to Shagyong village requires a 4WD vehicle due to challenging terrain.

name: Lachen Thangu Monastery
adress: Lachen, Sikkim, India
description: Established in the early 19th century by a Lama from Kham, Tibet, this Nyingma monastery serves as a spiritual center for the local community. It is affiliated with Lachen Monastery and is situated in the remote. This monastery is said to be in existence from the begining of the 19th century as a hermitage founded by a Lama from Kham, Tibet, who came and settled here. The Lama was an accomplished master and when he was criticised for getting married, he is said to have demonstrated his power by leaving a footprint on a stone slab, which is presently kept at Lachen Gonpa .Thangu Valley.  Thangu monastery is no doubt a branch monastery of Lachen Gonpa  and is located about 29 kms from its parent monastery on the way to Guru Dongmar Lake. It was earlier a small Lhakhang (Temple) built in 1974 by the people of Lachen. However, in the year 2003, they rebuilt a bigger Monastery withGovernment assistance. The history of this monastery is quite unique. It is said that in summer months, the people of Lachen along with their household shift to Thangu, the altitude of which is higher than Lachen, for cultivation in summer and the periodical ceremonies falling within these months are performed in Thangu monastery. When winter approaches, they return to Lachen. This practice is still followed to this day.
attractions: the trekking opportunities to places like Green Lake and Lhonak Valley.
transport: An Inner Line Permit is mandatory for visiting Lachen and must be arranged through a tour operator in Sikkim. Many travelers choose to stay a night in Gangtok to make the necessary arrangements for their permits and transportation to Lachen.  travel by road, with Bagdogra Airport (near Siliguri) and New Jalpaiguri Railway Station (NJP) in West Bengal being the nearest air and rail connections, respectively

name: Silem Phagyal Monastery
adress: Silem, South Sikkim, India
description: Established in 1967, Silem Phagyal Monastery, also known as Tashi Dargyeling, is a prominent Nyingma Buddhist monastery in South Sikkim. It serves as a spiritual center for the local community and is affiliated with the Serdup Choling Monastery, located in the same region.
opening: Open during daylight hours; specific timings may vary.
attractions: Rabong Monastery, Tendong Hill, Maenam Hill, Ralang Monastery.
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi to Ravangla (~65 km, 23 hours). From Ravangla, Silem is approximately 6 km away and can be reached by a short drive or trek. Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~60 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~120 km).

name: Barphog Monastery
adress: North Sikkim, India
description: Barphog Chhodub Dargyeling Monastery is another Nyingmapa Gonpa of Dzongu area in North Sikkim. The Gonpa can be reached by a steep climb from Lingdong. It was built in 1957 A.D. by the lamas with the help of the area public to cater the religious needs of the people. Now it functions all the ceremonies throughout the year. Presently the Gonpa has 30 monks with Lhazang Lepcha as Caretaker. Barphog Monastery, also known as Chhodub Dargyeling Monastery, was established in 1957. It follows the Nyingma tradition of Tibetan Buddhism and serves as a spiritual center for the local community.
attractions: Dzongu Valley, Tholung Monastery, Lingzya Waterfall, Keushong Lake
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via ManganDzongu Road (~65 km, 34 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~70 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~120 km). Access to Barphog village requires a 4WD vehicle due to challenging terrain.

name: Nage Monastery
adress: Naga, North Sikkim, India
description: This Monastery is known as Ugen Sangdoling and is situated at Naga, North Sikkim. Earlier, people were conducting Mani Puja in a Kutcha House. It follows Nyingmapa Sect of Mahayana Buddhism. The monastery was built in 1937 A.D. by the devotees on the advice of one High Lama Kyabgon Batrul Rinpoche who during his tour to northern Sikkim, halted at Naga Manilhakhang. Seeing a pitiable condition of the Lhakhang, the Rinpoche advised them to build a stable monastery since there is no monastery nearby Naga area where devotees could go to offer prayer. Presently, a bigger Monastery is being built  and is yet to be completed.
opening: Open during daylight hours; specific timings may vary.
attractions: Naga Devata Temples, Naga Devata Temples, Naga Devata Temples
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via ManganDzongu Road (~65 km, 34 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~70 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~120 km). Access to Naga village requires a 4WD vehicle due to challenging terrain.

name: Kabi Monastery
adress: Kabi, North Sikkim, India
description: Kabi Sanga Dargyeling monastery is located about 25 kilometers away from Gangtok on the way to North Sikkim.This Monastery was started as a Manilhakhang or nun Monastery built by the public in 1911 for the purpose of periodical pujas.The same was upgraded into full-fledged monastery in 2003 by the State Government. The monastery belongs to Nyingmapa Sect of Sikkimese Buddhism and functions all the major holy ceremonies. A famous place of worship called Kabi Longtsok or the oath taking place of the blood brotherhood of Bhutia-Lepcha is situated just above it along the North Sikkim Highway.
attractions: tranquil fores, life-size statues of the Lepcha and Bhutia chieftains
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via the North Sikkim Highway (~17 km, approximately 40 minutes). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~35 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~120 km).

name: Singchit Ngadag Monastery
adress: Singhik, North Sikkim, India
description: Established in 1890 by followers of Ngadag Rinchhen Gon, the 3rd successor of Ngadag Sempa Chepo, Singchit Ngadag Monastery is a significant Nyingma Buddhist site. The original structure was repaired in 1923, and a new monastery is currently under construction nearby
attractions: Singhik Viewpoint, Mangan, treks to places like the Jhandi
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via ManganDzongu Road (~70 km, 34 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~80 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~120 km). Access to Singhik village requires a 4WD vehicle due to challenging terrain.

name: Gor Monastery
adress: Gor, North Sikkim, India
description:
Established in 2002, Gor Rinchen Khando Monastery is a newly built Nyingma Buddhist monastery situated in the Dzongu area of North Sikkim. Prior to its construction, lamas and devotees had to walk long distances to attend pujas and ceremonies. The monastery serves as a spiritual center for the local community. 
Gor Monastery is another newly built monastery in the Dzongu area of North Sikkim. Earlier, lamas and devotees had to walk a long distance to attend pujas at Tareng Monastery, which was known earlier as Gor-Tareng Gonpa.Later they broke away, built a monastery in their own village in the year 2002, and named as Gor Rinchen Khando Gonpa. The monastery belongs to Nyingmapa Sect and performs all the ceremonies.
attractions: Khando Sangphuk, the Pelling area, the Namchi Buddha Park, and the Temi Tea Garden
transport: Khando Sangphuk, the Pelling area, the Namchi Buddha Park, and the Temi Tea Garden

name: Malam Monastery
adress: Tareng, North Sikkim, India
description: Established in 1928, Malam Monastery, also known as Tareng Gonpa Dargye Choling, is a Nyingma Buddhist monastery situated in the Dzongu region of North Sikkim. It serves as a spiritual center for the local community and is dedicated to the practice and preservation of Tibetan Buddhism.
attractions: high-altitude valleys and lakes of North Sikkim, including Yumthang Valley, Zero Point, and Gurudongmar Lake
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via ManganDzongu Road (~65 km, 34 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~70 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~120 km). Access to Tareng village requires a 4WD vehicle due to challenging terrain.

name: Tsawang Choling Monastery
adress: situated in the Dzongu region of North Sikkim
description: This monastery lies near Phensang monastery North Sikkim and follows Nyingmapa Sect.History: It is said that during the time of Chhogyal Tsugphud Namgyal (1785-1861), a Lama named Lody Gyaltsen or Tsipa Lama came from Phensang to Tsawang village with his pupil for  retreat courses where he built a small Shrine to serve his purpose. Later, a Nun called- Ani Khando used this shrine for similar purposes. Thereafter, two Nuns known as Ani Choying Ongmoo and Ani Kunga reconstructed this Lhakhang in bigger shape in fire-mouse year of 16th Rabjung cycle year, which corresponds to 1936 A.D. and named as Tsawang Ani Gonpa. Later, Lama Chonyi took over the charge of this monastery after the demise of Nuns. Presently the monastery is named Tsawang Choling Gonpa and being run by the lamas. The monastery presently has an enrolment of 55 monks and functions all the ceremonies throughout the year.
attractions: , Tholung Monastery, Lingzya Waterfall, Keushong Lake
transport: From Gangtok, take a jeep or taxi via ManganDzongu Road (~65 km, 34 hours). Nearest airport: Pakyong Airport (~70 km). Nearest railway station: New Jalpaiguri (NJP) Railway Station (~120 km). Access to Tsawang Choling requires a 4WD vehicle due to challenging terrain.

name: Doling Monastery
adress: Situated in the serene town of Ravangla, South Sikkim, India. The monastery is on a small hillock in the Barfung Village, about 6 km from Ravangla town. It is part of the Nyingma sect of Tibetan Buddhism.
description: A peaceful and spiritual site surrounded by lush forests with a stunning backdrop of the Himalayas. The monastery features vibrant murals, intricate thangka paintings, and a tranquil atmosphere enhanced by chanting monks and fluttering prayer flags. It's known for housing a sacred stone with a footprint believed to be from a horse of the deity Khangchendzonga and another of Lama Dorjelingpa. The monastery was founded in 1718 A.D. and has been rebuilt several times due to earthquake damage.
attractions:
In Ravangla:
- Buddha Park (Tathagata Tsal): A park with a 130-foot statue of Lord Buddha.
- Ralang Monastery: One of the most sacred monasteries in Sikkim.
- Bon Monastery: The only non-Buddhist monastery in the region.
- Borong Hot Springs: Known for their therapeutic properties.
- Temi Tea Garden: A large tea plantation on the slopes of Tendong Hills.
- Maenam Hill: A popular spot for trekking.
- Rayong Sunrise Viewpoint: Offers panoramic views of the sunrise over the Himalayan peaks.
transport:
By Road: From Gangtok, Ravangla is about 65 km away. Taxis and local buses are available from major cities in Sikkim to Ravangla. From Ravangla, you can hire a cab or take a local bus to the monastery. There is also a famous trekking route from the Barfung Retreat that involves climbing over 500 stairs through a forest.
By Train: The nearest railway station is New Jalpaiguri (NJP) in West Bengal, approximately 122 km from Ravangla. From there, you can hire a taxi to Ravangla.
By Air: The nearest airport is Bagdogra Airport (IXB) in West Bengal, and from there you can take a taxi to Ravangla.

name: Yangang Gonpa
adress: Situated in the town of Yangang, in the Namchi district of South Sikkim, India. The monastery is located in a peaceful, natural setting amidst lush greenery and rolling hills.
description: A serene retreat that reflects the deep-rooted Buddhist heritage of the region. The monastery is part of the larger cultural and spiritual landscape of Yangang, a town known for its natural beauty and tranquility. It is one of several monasteries in the area, including the Gurung and Lepcha Monasteries. The exact date of its founding is not widely documented, but a Reddit post indicates it was inaugurated on December 30, 2016
attractions:
- Maenam Hill (Bhaley-Dhunga): A popular trekking spot offering panoramic views. The area is also home to the Bhaleydhunga Ropeway, the longest in India, which provides a stunning aerial view of the Himalayas.
- Neya Khola Falls: The highest waterfall
transport:
By Air: The nearest airport is Bagdogra Airport (IXB) in West Bengal (approx. 125 km away). From there, you can hire a taxi to Yangang.
By Train: The nearest major railway station is New Jalpaiguri (NJP) in West Bengal (approx. 110 km away). Taxis and shared vehicles are available for hire to Yangang.
By Road: Yangang is well-connected by road to major towns in Sikkim and West Bengal. You can take a taxi from cities like Gangtok (about 72 km away) or Namchi (about 25 km away).

name: Ben Monastery
adress: Situated on the outskirts of Pelling in West Sikkim, India, Ben Monastery is perched on a hilltop offering commanding views of the surrounding valleys and the Himalayan mountain range. It is located near the road leading to Yuksom.
description: Ben Monastery, also known as Pemayangtse Monastery, is one of Sikkim's oldest and most significant monasteries, serving as the head monastery of the Nyingma sect. Founded by Lhatsun Chempo in 1705, it was later consecrated by Lama Lhatsun Jikme Pawo in 1717 A.D. The monastery features a three-storeyed building with a rich collection of statues and ancient Buddhist manuscripts. The central figure of worship is a magnificent statue of Guru Rinpoche (Padmasambhava). The monastery is a significant pilgrimage site and is renowned for its annual Chaam dance festival, where monks perform sacred masked dances. The history of the monastery is deeply intertwined with the royal family of Sikkim, as it was traditionally the monastery where all the Chogyals (monarchs) of Sikkim were consecrated.
attractions:
- Pemayangtse Monastery: Located a short distance away, this is one of Sikkim's most important monasteries.
- Rabdentse Ruins: The ruins of the former capital of Sikkim, offering a glimpse into the state's royal history.
- Pelling Skywalk and Chenre
transport:

name: Bon Monastery (Kagyed Gompa)
adress: Situated in the village of Kewzing, near Ravangla in South Sikkim, India. The monastery is located in a serene setting, surrounded by lush forests and a tranquil atmosphere.
description: A unique and significant monastery of the Bon sect, a pre-Buddhist religion of Tibet. Its history dates back to 1980, when it was founded by the XIVth Bon-po Bha-ra-Kha-Pa Lama. The monastery is the only one of its kind in Sikkim and is a testament to the diverse religious landscape of the region. It is known for its traditional Bon architecture and houses a collection of religious artifacts, manuscripts, and colorful murals. The monastery is a place for the community to gather for religious ceremonies, festivals, and cultural events.
attractions:
- Buddha Park (Tathagata Tsal): A park with a giant statue of Lord Buddha, a significant spiritual landmark.
- Ralang Monastery: One of the most sacred monasteries in Sikkim, known for its sacred relics and vibrant festivals.

transport:
By Road: The monastery is easily accessible by road from Ravangla town by local taxi or on foot. Ravangla is well-connected by road to major towns in Sikkim, such as Gangtok (approx. 65 km away), Namchi, and Pelling.

name: Yangang (general)
adress: A small town in the Namchi district of South Sikkim, India. It is situated amidst rolling hills and dense forests, and its strategic location makes it a gateway to other popular destinations like Ravangla and the Temi Tea Garden.
description: Yangang is a developing town that is gaining prominence as a tourist and educational hub. Historically, it is known as the birthplace of the former Chief Minister of Sikkim, Pawan Kumar Chamling. The town is rich in natural beauty and is an ideal destination for those seeking tranquility away from the hustle and bustle of city life. The landscape is a picturesque blend of terraced fields, lush vegetation, and diverse flora. It's a great place for birdwatching due to its proximity to the Maenam Wildlife Sanctuary. Future developments include a university campus, a skywalk, and a ropeway to Bhaley Dhunga, promising to further boost its tourism appeal.
attractions:
- Maenam Hill (Bhaley-Dhunga): A popular trekking spot that offers panoramic views of the Himalayas and the Teesta River valley. The upcoming Bhaleydhunga Ropeway is set to be the longest in India, offering a stunning aerial experience.

name: Dubde (Dubdi) Monastery
adress: Situated on a hilltop above the town of Yuksom in West Sikkim, India. It can only be reached by trekking through a dense forest.
description: Considered the oldest monastery in Sikkim. Its history dates back to 1701, when it was founded by Lhatsun Namkha Jigme, one of the three lamas who consecrated the first Chogyal (monarch) of Sikkim. The monastery is a small, two-storey stone structure with an elaborately painted interior. It houses statues of the three founding lamas and a collection of ancient manuscripts and texts. The name "Dubdi" means "the hermit's cell," and it is a peaceful retreat for meditation.
attractions:
- Norbugang Chorten: The coronation throne of the first Chogyal of Sikkim.
- Kartok Monastery: Another historic monastery in Yuksom.

name: Sanga Choeling Monastery
adress: Perched on a ridge opposite Pemayangtse Monastery near the town of Pelling in West Sikkim, India. It can be reached only by a 4 km walk from Upper Pelling.
description: One of the oldest monasteries in Sikkim, founded by Lhatsun Chempo in the 17th century. The name "Sanga Choeling" means "the place of secret spells." It is an important pilgrimage site for Buddhists, particularly of the Nyingma sect. The monastery is a beautiful two-storey structure with intricate murals, ancient clay idols, and statues of Buddhist deities. It is known for its peaceful and serene ambiance and offers a magnificent view of the Himalayan ranges.

name: Pemayangtse Monastery
adress: Located on a hilltop near Pelling in West Sikkim, India, commanding a magnificent view of the Himalayan ranges.
description: One of the oldest and premier monasteries of Sikkim, founded by Lhatsun Chempo in the 17th century. It was originally established as a monastery for "pure monks" (Ta-sang), and this practice is still retained. It follows the Nyingma Order of Tibetan Buddhism. The monastery houses a magnificent wooden sculpture depicting Guru Rinpoche's heavenly palace, Sangtok-Palri, a masterpiece crafted by the late Dungzin Rimpoche. It also contains numerous religious idols and ancient objects of worship. The head lama of this monastery had the unique privilege of anointing the Chogyals of Sikkim with holy water.
opening: Open mainly during MarchApril and SeptemberOctober for specific festivals; otherwise access is restricted.

By Train: The nearest railway station is New Jalpaiguri (NJP) in West Bengal (approx. 145 km).

name: Tashiding Monastery
adress: Situated on a heart-shaped hill between the Rathong Chu and Rangeet Rivers in Tashiding, West Sikkim, India.
description: Considered the most sacred and holiest monastery in Sikkim. Its history is tied to a legend where Guru Padmasambhava shot an arrow into the sky, and the monastery was built where it landed. It was founded in 1641 by Ngadak Sempa Chempo Punshok Rigzing of the Nyingma sect. The monastery is part of a sacred pilgrimage circuit and is believed to have a special power to cleanse sins. It is famous for the annual Bumchu festival, where devotees partake in holy water from a sacred vase.

name: Silnon Monastery
adress: Located in the village of Silnon, in the Namchi district of South Sikkim, India.
description: A historically significant monastery of the Nyingma sect, specifically the Jhang-Ter Lineage. Its history is subject to some debate, but it is widely believed to have been founded in 1716 A.D. by Ngadag Rinchhen Gon, the grandson of Saint Ngadag Sempa Chhenpo. The present structure was built in 1992, replacing the old one. The monastery is said to have been built upon a pond, which was considered an auspicious site. It contains statues of Buddha and the Eight Bodhisattvas. A nearby cave is believed to be the abode of a serpent deity.
attractions:
- Temi Tea Garden: Sikkim's only tea estate, known for its scenic beauty.

name: Khachoedpalri Monastery
adress: Situated near the sacred Khecheopalri Lake in West Sikkim, India. The monastery is surrounded by hills and dense forests.
description: A significant monastery with a rich history intertwined with the sacred Khecheopalri Lake. The site is a part of the "Demazong" (valley of rice) pilgrimage circuit. The monastery was established to oversee the spiritual activities around the lake, which is believed to be a "wish-fulfilling" lake. It is said that the lake was formed by the goddess Tara to provide a sacred place for meditation. The monastery has been a center of Buddhist learning and meditation for centuries.

name: Rinchenpung (Rinchenpong) Monastery
adress: Perched on a mountain trail above the twin villages of Rinchenpong and Kaluk in West Sikkim, India, offering a panoramic view of the surrounding mountains.
description: A significant monastery of the Nyingma sect, founded in 1730 by the Ngadakpa Lama. It is the third oldest monastery in Sikkim. The monastery is famous for housing a rare statue of Ati Buddha (Adi-Buddha) in the Yab-Yum position, which is a symbolic representation of the union of male and female principles. The monastery also serves as a training center for lamas. The entrance is lined with colorful prayer flags and prayer wheels.
attractions:
- Poison Pokhri (Poisoned Lake): A symbolic site of resistance against a British invasion.
- Rishum Monastery: Another 18th-century monastery located nearby.

name: Melli-Atsing Monastery
adress: Located in the village of Melli-Atsing in Sikkim, India. The exact location is not widely publicized, but it is within the Melli area.
description: A Buddhist monastery of the Nyingma sect. Its history dates back to 1740, when it was founded by Lama Dudjom Yeshey Nyingpo. The monastery is a small but significant religious site for the local community. It is known for its peaceful atmosphere and serves as a place for religious ceremonies and meditation.

name: Lhuntse Monastery
adress: The term "Lhuntse Monastery" is commonly associated with a few different monasteries in the Lhuntse district of Bhutan. The most notable are Lhuntse Dzong and Jangchubling Monastery. Lhuntse is a remote district in northeastern Bhutan.
description: The district of Lhuntse is known for its spiritual significance and historical ties to the Bhutanese royal family. The main monastery is the Lhuntse Dzong, a fortress-monastery built in 1654 by Trongsa Penlop Chogyal Minjur Tempa. It serves as both an administrative and religious center. Another significant monastery is Jangchubling Monastery, founded in the 18th century by Pekar Gyatso. It has a historical connection to the first King's daughter, who lived there as a nun. The monasteries in Lhuntse are known for their traditional Bhutanese architecture, intricate murals, and collection of sacred artifacts.
attractions:
- Lhuntse Dzong: A historic fortress-monastery overlooking the Kurichu River.
- Kilung Lhakhang: A sacred temple that houses a legendary chain used to recapture a statue.
- Jangchubling Monastery: An 18th-century monastery accessible by a feeder r
transport:
By Road: Lhuntse is one of the most isolated districts in Bhutan, accessible by road from Mongar (approx. 77 km). Taxis can be hired from major towns in Bhutan like Thimphu and Paro.
By Air: The nearest airport is Yongphulla Airport (YON) in eastern Bhutan, which has domestic flights. The nearest international airport is Paro International Airport (PBH) in western Bhutan.

name: Chakung Monastery
adress: Situated in the village of Chakung in West Sikkim, India. The monastery is a serene spiritual retreat nestled amidst rolling hills.
description: A significant monastery with a rich history of the Nyingma sect of Tibetan Buddhism. Its history dates back to 1874 A.D. The original structure was badly damaged in an earthquake, and a new monastery was erected in 2009. It is known for its peaceful atmosphere and intricate murals.

name: Okhery Monastery
adress: Situated in the serene village of Okhrey in West Sikkim, India. The monastery, named Ugen Thon Meon Ling, is located on a hilltop and is known for its peaceful surroundings.
description: A small but charming monastery following the Nyingmapa sect of Tibetan Buddhism. It was built in 1950. The monastery is a significant spiritual center for the local community, which consists primarily of Sherpa and Bhutia households. It is a place for meditation and religious practices, offering a quiet retreat from the busy world.

name: Aden Wolung Monastery
adress: Located in a remote area of West Sikkim, near Sombarey.
description: A Nyingmapa monastery founded in 1913 A.D. by a Sherpa Lama named Pema on his own land. The original structure was rebuilt with government aid between 1991 and 1995. It follows the Jhangter lineage of the Nyingmapa Sect and is presently under the spiritual guidance of Ven. Serdup Dungzin Lodoe Chophel Rinpoche.

name: Hungri Monastery
adress: Situated in West Sikkim, between the villages of Silnon and Yuksom.
description: A monastery of the Nyingmapa sect, following the Minling lineage. It was initially established by Lepcha Lamas with a small Lhakhang. The present structure, a branch of the Pemayangtse Monastery, was erected in 1922 A.D. by Tamatam Lama. It is one of the few monasteries that retains its original stone walls. An interesting feature is a small stone altar on the exterior wall dedicated to the Serpent God Lunag Thana Tenzing. The present structure was damaged by the earthquake in 2011 and is in need of restoration.
attractions: #NAME?

name: Nubling Monastery
adress: Located in the remote and inaccessible Nubri Valley, high on the southern slopes of the Himalayas, on the border of Tibet and Nepal. This monastery is not located in Sikkim.
description: A unique and ancient Buddhist sanctuary of the Nyingmapa (old school) sect. Its history dates back over five hundred years. The monastery is in a remote location, a hidden land called Beyul, and is a haven for pure Dharma. It has a community of monks and nuns who are educated equally. The monastery operates on a very meager budget and is financially supported by donations from around the world to continue its mission of preserving Buddhist teachings.
attractions: #NAME?
transport: By Trek: The monastery is located in a very remote area and can only be reached by trekking. The trek to the Nubri Valley is part of the Manaslu Circuit Trek in Nepal, and access requires special permits. It is not easily accessible by road.

name: Sri Badam Monastery
adress: Situated in the forest village of Sribadam in West Sikkim, India. The village is at an altitude of 2,100 meters and is known for its spiritual significance.
description: Known as Nyingmapa Palyul Dechen Gawelling Monastery, it is a renowned meditation center and is believed to have a healing capacity for various ailments through religious rituals. It is famous for its annual Phurpa Tordo Puja and Cham Dance (Mask Dance) performed in December. The monastery is an important spiritual hub for the local community, which consists of Bhutia, Subba, and Gurung populations.

name: Bermiok Monastery
adress: Situated in the village of Bermiok in South Sikkim, India. It is a part of the Karma Kagyupa sect of Buddhism.
description: Known as Bermiok Wosel Choling Gonpa, this monastery was built in 1873 A.D. through the combined efforts of the lamas and laypeople of the area. It has a history of resilience, having been rebuilt twice, in 1954, after being damaged by earthquakes. The monastery serves as a spiritual center for the local community and is a testament to their dedication to their faith.

The monastery is known for its aesthetic appearance and serves as a spiritual center for the Gurung community. It has a residential facility for novice monks who are studying under a senior monk.

name: Rinchen Choling Tamu Monastery
adress: Situated in the village of Meghi Dara, Rinchenpong, in the Gyalshing district of West Sikkim, India.
description: A significant monastery with a rich history of the Tamang community. The monastery was recently renovated and inaugurated by the Chief Minister of Sikkim in December 2024. The original history of the monastery is tied to the local community, with "Tamu" being the Tamang word for Gurung. The monastery serves as a vital religious and cultural center for the Tamang community in the region.
